_note: Heaviest chapter. Arts 200 (grounded conductor identification) and 285 (SPDs) inline. Arts 210, 215, 220, 230, 240, 250 have dedicated per-article files — only pointer entries here.

articles.article_title: Use and Identification of Grounded Conductors
articles.scope: Use and identification of the grounded conductor — colour coding, terminal identification, polarity at switching/protective devices.

key_sections.title: Means of Identifying Grounded Conductors
key_sections.summary: ≤6 AWG: continuous white or gray insulation, or three white stripes. >6 AWG: white at terminations only OR colour distinct + white tape/marking at every termination

key_sections.title: Use of Insulation that is White or Gray or has Three Continuous White Stripes
key_sections.summary: White/gray/striped insulation reserved for grounded conductor — exceptions for reidentification in cable assemblies, dimmer-controlled circuits, etc.

common_errors: Using white tape on a black conductor as the only identification on a high-voltage feeder — for >6 AWG only the entire termination must be re-identified; on circuits ≤6 AWG, white insulation is required (not tape)
Reusing a white conductor as a hot leg in a 240 V two-wire circuit without re-identification per 200.7
articles.divergence_notes: NEC requires white or gray for grounded conductor (200.6); IEC uses blue (BS EN 60446 / IEC 60446) for the neutral. NEC permits orange to identify the high-leg in 4-wire delta systems; IEC has no equivalent (delta neutral systems are rare in IEC jurisdictions).
articles.usage_notes: On US/IEC bi-jurisdiction projects, document the colour code in the design narrative — defaulting to either standard alone causes confusion at site.

articles.article_title: Overvoltage Protection (formerly Art 285 SPDs, reorganised in 2020 to Art 242)
articles.scope: Surge protective devices (SPDs) — selection, location, application, marking. SPD types and classes per UL 1449. NOTE: Article number 285 used in NEC 2017; reorganised to 242 in NEC 2020 onwards. References to 'Art 285' in older documents map to 'Art 242' under NEC 2023.

key_sections.title: Listing
key_sections.summary: SPDs must be listed to UL 1449

key_sections.title: Number Required
key_sections.summary: Where required by AHJ or design — typically Type 1 or 2 at service entrance, Type 3 at sensitive equipment

key_sections.title: Location
key_sections.summary: Type 1 ahead of service disconnect; Type 2 on load side of service; Type 3 within 30 ft of protected equipment

key_sections.title: Type 1, 2, 3, 4 SPDs
key_sections.summary: Type 1 = supply-side of service disconnect; Type 2 = load-side; Type 3 = point-of-use; Type 4 = component SPDs

common_errors: Installing a Type 2 SPD on the supply side of the service disconnect — only Type 1 permitted there
Failing to coordinate Type 2 (8/20 µs nominal discharge) with upstream Type 1 (10/350 µs lightning impulse) — see ocpd-coordination.json for the let-through-voltage coordination
Using Article 285 in design references with NEC 2020+ — the article was renumbered to 242
articles.divergence_notes: NEC SPD types (1, 2, 3, 4 per UL 1449) correspond loosely to IEC SPD types (Type 1 / 2 / 3 per IEC 61643-11) but the test waveforms differ — UL 1449 short-circuit current test vs IEC 8/20 µs and 10/350 µs impulses. NEC does not yet mandate SPDs universally (AHJ-dependent); IEC 60364-4-44 AMD2:2018 made SPDs effectively mandatory at the main DB in all new installations unless a risk analysis justifies omission.
articles.usage_notes: Cite Art 242 (NEC 2023) in new design documents; legacy 'Art 285' references in client/utility specs map to Art 242. Always coordinate Type 1 + Type 2 let-through voltages so Type 2 protects the panelboard's downstream equipment.

articles.scope: Branch-circuit ratings, GFCI/AFCI requirements, receptacle outlet rules.
Feeder load, ampacity, GFP for feeders ≥1000 A.

articles.article_title: Branch-Circuit, Feeder, and Service Load Calculations
articles.scope: Standard and Optional load calculation methods (220.40, 220.82).
Service drop/lateral, service conductor ampacity, disconnect, GFP at services.
OCPD types, interrupting rating, conductor protection, tap rules, series ratings.
System and equipment grounding, grounding electrode system, GEC/EGC sizing, bonding.
